lists.title: French A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in communicative order: subject pronouns, noun gender and number, both article families, être and avoir, the three verb groups in the present, negation, il y a, questions, and adjective agreement.

points.title: Subject pronouns (je, tu, il…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: The subject pronouns are je (I), tu (you, informal), il/elle (he/she), on (one/we, informal), nous (we), vous (you, formal or plural), ils/elles (they). Vous doubles as the polite singular — use it with strangers. On is how spoken French usually says 'we': on mange à midi.
points.culture_note: Choosing tu or vous is a real social decision. When unsure, start with vous and wait to be invited to tutoyer.

drills.translation: I am from Paris.
You are my friend.
She is my sister.
We are students.
They are from Spain.
You are very kind.
He works in Paris.
They are my friends (all women).
I speak a little French.
Where do you live?
He works in a bank.
She sings very well.
We live in Lyon.
Do you speak English?
They play football.
They (f.) arrive tomorrow.

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Name people and things, one or many
points.explanation: Every noun is masculine or feminine — learn each noun with its gender, because little about the word itself tells you. The plural usually adds a silent -s (livre → livres); since it isn't pronounced, the article carries the plural out loud. Endings give clues: -tion and -té are usually feminine, -age and -eau usually masculine.

drills.translation: I have two books.
The houses are beautiful.
I have three sisters.
The gardens are big.
I have two cats at home.
The cars are new.

points.title: Definite articles (le / la / les)
points.function: Point to a specific thing
points.explanation: Le before masculine nouns (le livre), la before feminine (la maison), les for any plural (les livres). Before a vowel or silent h, le and la both shrink to l' (l'école). French uses the definite article far more than English — also for general statements: j'aime le café (I like coffee).

drills.translation: The house is big.
The book is new.
The children are playing outside.
The coffee is hot.
The door is open.
The dogs are in the garden.

points.title: Indefinite articles (un / une / des)
points.function: Mention something for the first time
points.explanation: Un before masculine nouns (un chien), une before feminine (une maison), and — unlike English — a plural indefinite des (des amis, 'some friends'). Des is required where English just drops the article: j'ai des questions.

drills.translation: I have a dog.
It is a big house.
He has an idea.
We have (some) friends in Lyon.
It is a good book.
She is buying (some) apples.

points.title: Être — present (je suis, tu es…)
points.function: Say who or what someone is
points.explanation: Être (to be) is wildly irregular and everywhere: je suis, tu es, il/elle est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils/elles sont. Professions take no article after être: elle est professeur.

drills.translation: I am a student.
You are very tall.
She is a teacher.
We are friends.
Are you French?
They are at home.
I am from Marseille.
Are you a doctor?
My father is a teacher.
We are ready.
You are very kind.
My parents are from Nice.

points.title: Avoir — present (j'ai faim)
points.function: Say what you have — and how you feel
points.explanation: Avoir (to have): j'ai, tu as, il/elle a, nous avons, vous avez, ils/elles ont. French uses avoir where English uses 'to be' for states: avoir faim (be hungry), avoir soif (be thirsty), avoir vingt ans (be twenty), avoir raison (be right).
points.culture_note: Never say 'je suis vingt ans' — age is something you HAVE in French.

drills.translation: I am twenty years old.
Are you hungry?
She has a cat.
We have a question.
You are right.
They are thirsty.
I have a small question.
Do you have a pen?
My brother has a dog.
We have time.
Do you have children?
They have a big house.

points.title: Present tense of -er verbs
points.function: Talk about what people do
points.explanation: Ninety percent of French verbs end in -er and share one pattern. Drop -er, add: -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent (parler → je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent). Four of those six forms sound identical — the pronoun does the work in speech.

drills.translation: We speak French.
She works in an office.
Do you like chocolate?
They play football.
Do you live in Paris?
I close the door.
I speak a little French.
I work in a hospital.
What time do you arrive?
We prepare dinner together.
Do you listen to the radio?
My friends study Spanish.

points.title: -ir and -re verbs (finir, vendre)
points.function: Finish, choose, wait, sell — the other two families
points.explanation: -ir verbs like finir: je finis, tu finis, il finit, nous finissons (note the -iss- in the plural), vous finissez, ils finissent. -re verbs like vendre: je vends, tu vends, il vend, nous vendons, vous vendez, ils vendent. Attendre (wait), entendre (hear), and choisir (choose) follow these patterns.

references.title: Wikipedia: French conjugation — second and third groups

drills.sentence: Je {{answer}} mon travail à six heures.
drills.translation: I finish my work at six o'clock.
We are waiting for the train.
They finish their homework.
She sells flowers at the market.
Do you hear the music?
Are you choosing a dessert?
I am waiting for the bus.
Are you finishing your homework?
He is selling his car.
We sell our vegetables at the market.
Are you waiting for the train here?
They are choosing a gift.

points.title: Negation with ne … pas
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation: Negation wraps the verb in a frame: ne + verb + pas. Je ne parle pas anglais. Before a vowel, ne becomes n': je n'aime pas. After a negation, un/une/des become de: je n'ai pas de chien.
points.culture_note: In casual speech the ne often disappears — je parle pas. Learn the full frame first; drop it later like the locals.

drills.translation: I don't speak English.
drills.hint: the opening half of the frame

drills.translation: He doesn't work on Sundays.
drills.hint: the closing half of the frame

drills.translation: We are not tired.
drills.hint: the opening half of the frame

drills.translation: She doesn't eat meat.
drills.hint: the closing half of the frame

drills.translation: Aren't you eating?
drills.hint: the opening half of the frame

drills.translation: You don't dance?
drills.hint: the closing half of the frame

points.title: Il y a — there is / there are
points.function: Say what exists or is available
points.explanation: Il y a covers both 'there is' and 'there are' — it never changes for plural: il y a un livre, il y a deux livres. Negative: il n'y a pas de… (no article, just de). Question: est-ce qu'il y a… ?

drills.sentence: Il y {{answer}} un livre sur la table.
drills.translation: There is a book on the table.
drills.hint: the verb of the fixed phrase

drills.sentence: {{answer}} y a deux cafés dans la rue.
drills.translation: There are two cafés in the street.

drills.sentence: Il {{answer}} a beaucoup de monde ici.
drills.translation: There are a lot of people here.
drills.hint: the little word in the middle

drills.sentence: Il y a {{answer}} chat dans le jardin.
drills.translation: There is a cat in the garden.
Is there any bread?
drills.hint: the verb of the fixed phrase

drills.translation: There is no milk.
drills.hint: after a negation, articles become this

points.title: Question words (où, comment, quand…)
points.function: Ask about places, people, time, and reasons
points.explanation: Où (where), comment (how), quand (when), que/qu' (what), pourquoi (why), combien (how much/many), qui (who). Three ways to build a question: intonation (tu viens ?), est-ce que (est-ce que tu viens ?), or inversion (viens-tu ?) — est-ce que is the everyday workhorse.

drills.translation: Where do you live?
What is your name?
When is your birthday?
What are you watching?
How many brothers do you have?
Why are you crying?

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.function: Describe people and things correctly
points.explanation: Adjectives agree with their noun: add -e for feminine (grand → grande), -s for plural (grands, grandes). Some change more: blanc → blanche, nouveau → nouvelle, gentil → gentille. Most adjectives FOLLOW the noun (une maison blanche), but a small frequent set precedes it (grand, petit, bon, nouveau, joli).

drills.translation: The house is white.
The books are new.
My sister is very kind.
The coffee is hot.
The flowers are pretty.
The film is interesting.
The walls are white.
The doors are open.

points.title: Passé composé with avoir
points.function: Talk about completed past actions
points.explanation: The everyday past = a present-tense form of avoir + the past participle: j'ai mangé 'I ate / have eaten'. Regular participles: -er → -é (mangé), -ir → -i (fini), -re → -u (vendu). The auxiliary is conjugated (ai, as, a, avons, avez, ont); the participle stays the same (no agreement with the subject when the auxiliary is avoir).

drills.translation: Yesterday I ate at the restaurant.

drills.sentence: Ce matin, j'{{answer}} bu un café.
drills.translation: This morning I drank a coffee.
Have you finished your homework?

drills.sentence: Quand est-ce que tu {{answer}} vu ce film?
drills.translation: When did you see this film?
He lost his keys.
She sang a beautiful song.
We visited the museum.
This weekend we danced a lot.
Have you already booked the table?
What time did you have dinner?

drills.sentence: Les enfants {{answer}} joué dans le jardin.
drills.translation: The children played in the garden.

drills.sentence: Mes amis {{answer}} travaillé tout le week-end.
drills.translation: My friends worked all weekend.

points.title: Passé composé with être
points.function: Use être for movement and reflexive verbs in the past
points.explanation: A set of verbs of movement/change (aller, venir, partir, arriver, monter, descendre, naître, mourir, rester, tomber…) and all reflexive verbs form the past with être, not avoir: je suis allé 'I went'. With être the participle AGREES with the subject in gender and number: elle est allée, ils sont partis, elles sont venues.

drills.sentence: Hier soir, je {{answer}} allé au cinéma.
drills.translation: Last night I went to the cinema.

drills.sentence: Ce matin, je {{answer}} arrivé en retard.
drills.translation: This morning I arrived late.
Did you come to the party?
What time did you leave?
She stayed home.
The train has already left.
We got home very late.

drills.sentence: Ce week-end, nous {{answer}} sortis en ville.
drills.translation: This weekend we went out in town.
Did you come down by the stairs?
When were you born?
The guests arrived at noon.

drills.sentence: Mes sœurs {{answer}} montées dans le bus.
drills.translation: My sisters got on the bus.

points.title: The imperfect (l'imparfait)
points.function: Describe ongoing or habitual past actions
points.explanation: The imperfect describes a past that was ongoing, repeated, or a background state — English 'was …-ing' / 'used to'. Take the nous form of the present, drop -ons, add -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient: mangeais 'I was eating', finissait 'he used to finish'. être is irregular (j'étais). Contrast with the passé composé (one completed act).

drills.sentence: Chaque été, nous {{answer}} à la mer.
drills.translation: Every summer we used to go to the seaside.
When I was little, I used to read a lot.

drills.sentence: Qu'est-ce que tu {{answer}} quand je t'ai appelé?
drills.translation: What were you doing when I called you?

drills.sentence: Mon grand-père {{answer}} des histoires chaque soir.
drills.translation: My grandfather used to tell stories every evening.
The children were playing in the park.

drills.sentence: Avant, nous {{answer}} dans un petit appartement.
drills.translation: We used to live in a small apartment.

points.title: The future (le futur simple)
points.function: Talk about what will happen
points.explanation: The simple future adds -ai, -as, -a, -ons, -ez, -ont to the infinitive (dropping a final -e of -re verbs): je mangerai 'I will eat', il finira 'he will finish'. Some stems are irregular: être → ser-, avoir → aur-, aller → ir-, faire → fer-, venir → viendr-.

drills.translation: Tomorrow I will go to work early.

drills.sentence: Ce soir, nous {{answer}} un film ensemble.
drills.translation: Tonight we will watch a film together.

drills.sentence: Est-ce que tu {{answer}} avec nous en voyage?
drills.translation: Will you come with us on the trip?
Anna will buy the gift tomorrow.

drills.sentence: L'année prochaine, je {{answer}} du français chaque jour.
drills.translation: Next year I will do French every day.
My friends will be at the party.

points.title: Direct object pronouns (le, la, les…)
points.function: Replace the object with a short pronoun
points.explanation: A known direct object becomes a pronoun placed before the verb: le (him/it m.), la (her/it f.), les (them), me, te, nous, vous. Je le vois 'I see him', je les achète 'I'm buying them'. Before a vowel, me/te/le/la elide: je t'aime, je l'ai vu.

drills.sentence: Tu connais Marc? Oui, je {{answer}} connais bien.
drills.translation: Do you know Marc? Yes, I know him well.

drills.sentence: Où est Marie? Je ne {{answer}} vois pas.
drills.translation: Where is Marie? I don't see her.

drills.sentence: Ces livres sont bien; je {{answer}} lis maintenant.
drills.translation: These books are good; I'm reading them now.

drills.sentence: Si tu viens, je {{answer}} attends à la gare.
drills.translation: If you come, I'll wait for you at the station.
We're here; can you hear us?

drills.sentence: J'aime cette chanson; je {{answer}} écoute souvent.
drills.translation: I love this song; I listen to it often.

points.title: Indirect pronouns, y and en (lui, leur, y, en)
points.function: Replace 'to someone', a place, or a quantity
points.explanation: lui 'to him/her' and leur 'to them' replace à + a person: je lui parle 'I talk to him'. y replaces à + a thing or a place: j'y vais 'I go there'. en replaces de + something or a quantity: j'en veux 'I want some', j'en ai trois 'I have three of them'.

drills.sentence: Je téléphone à Paul; je {{answer}} téléphone ce soir.
drills.translation: I'm calling Paul; I'll call him tonight.

drills.sentence: Tu écris à tes parents? Oui, je {{answer}} écris souvent.
drills.translation: Do you write to your parents? Yes, I write to them often.

drills.sentence: Tu vas à Paris? Oui, j'{{answer}} vais demain.
drills.translation: Are you going to Paris? Yes, I'm going there tomorrow.

drills.sentence: Tu veux du café? Oui, j'{{answer}} veux bien.
drills.translation: Do you want some coffee? Yes, I'd like some.

drills.sentence: Des pommes? J'{{answer}} ai acheté un kilo.
drills.translation: Apples? I bought a kilo of them.

drills.sentence: Tu penses à ton examen? Oui, j'{{answer}} pense.
drills.translation: Are you thinking about your exam? Yes, I'm thinking about it.

points.title: Comparative & superlative (plus… que, le plus)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation: The comparative uses plus / moins / aussi + adjective + que 'than/as': plus grand que 'bigger than', aussi cher que 'as expensive as'. The superlative adds the definite article: le plus grand 'the biggest'. Irregular: bon → meilleur 'better', bien → mieux 'better (adv.)'.

drills.translation: Paris is bigger than Lyon.

drills.sentence: C'est le {{answer}} beau café de la ville.
drills.translation: It's the most beautiful café in town.

drills.sentence: Le français est {{answer}} difficile que l'espagnol pour moi.
drills.translation: French is harder than Spanish for me.
This cake is better than the other one.

drills.sentence: En été, les jours sont {{answer}} longs.
drills.translation: In summer the days are longer.

drills.sentence: La santé est {{answer}} importante que l'argent.
drills.translation: Health is more important than money.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (se laver)
points.function: Say the subject acts on itself
points.explanation: Reflexive verbs carry a pronoun matching the subject: me, te, se, nous, vous, se, before the verb. Je me lave 'I wash (myself)', il se lève 'he gets up', nous nous reposons 'we rest'. Many daily-routine verbs are reflexive. In the passé composé they take être.

drills.sentence: Chaque matin, je {{answer}} lève à sept heures.
drills.translation: Every morning I get up at seven.

drills.sentence: À quelle heure est-ce que tu {{answer}} couches?
drills.translation: What time do you go to bed?
She feels much better today.

drills.sentence: Nous {{answer}} reposons le dimanche à la maison.
drills.translation: We rest at home on Sundays.
Are you having fun at the party?

drills.sentence: Les enfants {{answer}} lavent les mains avant le repas.
drills.translation: The children wash their hands before the meal.

points.title: Partitive articles (du, de la, des)
points.function: Say 'some' of an uncountable thing
points.explanation: To talk about an unspecified quantity ('some'), French uses du (m.), de la (f.), de l' (before a vowel), des (pl.): je bois du café, je mange de la salade. After a negation, all become de/d': je ne bois pas de café. After an expression of quantity too: beaucoup de café.

drills.translation: In the morning I drink coffee.
We buy fruit at the market.
I don't drink wine.

drills.sentence: Il y a beaucoup {{answer}} monde ici.
drills.translation: There are a lot of people here.
She buys vegetables at the market.

drills.sentence: Tu prends {{answer}} sucre dans ton thé?
drills.translation: Do you take sugar in your tea?

points.title: Possessive & demonstrative adjectives (mon, ce…)
points.function: Say 'my / this' and agree with the noun
points.explanation: Possessives agree with the thing owned, not the owner: mon/ma/mes 'my', ton/ta/tes 'your', son/sa/ses 'his/her', notre/nos, votre/vos, leur/leurs. Before a feminine noun starting with a vowel, use mon/ton/son (mon amie). Demonstratives: ce/cet (m.), cette (f.), ces (pl.) 'this/these'.

drills.translation: My car is new.
Where is your phone?
His/her sister lives in Lyon.
This book is very interesting.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} maison est près de la mer.
drills.translation: This house is near the sea.
These children are playing in the garden.

points.title: The subjunctive (le subjonctif)
points.function: Express necessity, wish, emotion, and doubt
points.explanation: After expressions of necessity, wish, emotion, or doubt — il faut que, je veux que, je suis content que, bien que — the verb goes into the subjunctive. For most verbs, take the ils form of the present, drop -ent, add -e, -es, -e, -ions, -iez, -ent: que je parle, que nous finissions. Key irregulars: être (sois), avoir (aie), faire (fasse), aller (aille).

drills.sentence: Il faut que je {{answer}} mes devoirs ce soir.
drills.translation: I have to do my homework tonight.

drills.sentence: Je veux que tu {{answer}} à l'heure.
drills.translation: I want you to be on time.

drills.sentence: Il est important que nous {{answer}} attention.
drills.translation: It's important that we pay attention.
Although he is ill, he works.

drills.sentence: Je suis content que vous {{answer}} venir.
drills.translation: I'm glad you can come.

drills.sentence: Il faut qu'elle {{answer}} chez le médecin.
drills.translation: She has to go to the doctor.

points.title: The conditional (le conditionnel)
points.function: Say what you would do; be polite
points.explanation: The conditional = the future stem + the imperfect endings (-ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient): je mangerais 'I would eat', il viendrait 'he would come'. It softens requests (je voudrais 'I would like', pourriez-vous 'could you') and states hypotheticals.

drills.sentence: Je {{answer}} un café, s'il vous plaît.
drills.translation: I would like a coffee, please.
Could you help me for a moment?

drills.sentence: Il {{answer}} volontiers, mais il est occupé.
drills.translation: He would gladly come, but he's busy.
In your place, I would accept the offer.

drills.sentence: Nous {{answer}} bien vivre au bord de la mer.
drills.translation: We would love to live by the sea.
They would be happy to see you.

points.title: Two object pronouns (je le lui donne)
points.function: Combine 'it' and 'to him' before the verb
points.explanation: When a verb has two pronouns, they follow a fixed order before the verb: me/te/nous/vous come before le/la/les, which come before lui/leur, then y, then en: je te le donne 'I give it to you', je le lui donne 'I give it to him', il y en a 'there is some'.

drills.sentence: Ce livre? Je {{answer}} le prête volontiers.
drills.translation: This book? I'll gladly lend it to you.

drills.sentence: La lettre, je {{answer}} lui ai déjà envoyée.
drills.translation: The letter, I already sent it to him.

drills.sentence: Les clés? Il {{answer}} les a rendues hier.
drills.translation: The keys? He gave them back to us yesterday.

drills.sentence: Du gâteau? Il y {{answer}} a encore.
drills.translation: Some cake? There's still some.

drills.sentence: Ton adresse, donne-la-{{answer}}, s'il te plaît.
drills.translation: Your address, give it to me, please.

drills.sentence: Cette histoire, je {{answer}} la raconterai demain.
drills.translation: This story, I'll tell it to you tomorrow.

points.title: Relative pronouns (qui, que, où, dont)
points.function: Join two clauses into one
points.explanation: qui is the subject of the relative clause ('who/which' + verb): l'homme qui parle. que is the object ('whom/which/that' + subject): le livre que je lis. où means 'where/when': la ville où j'habite. dont replaces de + something ('whose/of which/about which'): le film dont je parle.

drills.translation: That's the man who lives next door.

drills.sentence: Le livre {{answer}} je lis est passionnant.
drills.translation: The book that I'm reading is gripping.

drills.sentence: Voici la ville {{answer}} je suis né.
drills.translation: Here is the town where I was born.

drills.sentence: C'est le film {{answer}} tout le monde parle.
drills.translation: That's the film everyone is talking about.

drills.sentence: Les amis {{answer}} viennent ce soir sont sympas.
drills.translation: The friends who are coming tonight are nice.

drills.sentence: La voiture {{answer}} il a achetée est rouge.
drills.translation: The car that he bought is red.

points.title: Near future & recent past (aller / venir de)
points.function: Say what's about to happen or just happened
points.explanation: The near future is aller (present) + infinitive: je vais partir 'I'm going to leave'. The recent past is venir de + infinitive: je viens d'arriver 'I've just arrived'. Both are extremely common in speech.

drills.sentence: Attends, je {{answer}} arriver dans cinq minutes.
drills.translation: Wait, I'm going to arrive in five minutes.
We're going to leave on holiday tomorrow.
He has just finished his work.
I've just received your message.
You're going to love this restaurant!
The children have just woken up.

points.title: The pluperfect (le plus-que-parfait)
points.function: Set one past action before another
points.explanation: The pluperfect ('had done') = the imperfect of avoir/être + the past participle: j'avais mangé 'I had eaten', elle était partie 'she had left'. It marks an action completed before another past moment. The être/avoir and agreement rules are the same as the passé composé.

drills.sentence: Quand tu es arrivé, j'{{answer}} déjà mangé.
drills.translation: When you arrived, I had already eaten.

drills.sentence: Le train {{answer}} déjà parti quand nous sommes arrivés.
drills.translation: The train had already left when we arrived.

drills.sentence: Je n'ai pas reconnu la maison; elle {{answer}} beaucoup changé.
drills.translation: I didn't recognize the house; it had changed a lot.
They had left before the rain.

drills.sentence: Nous {{answer}} déjà fini quand il a appelé.
drills.translation: We had already finished when he called.

drills.sentence: Elle m'a dit qu'elle {{answer}} oublié son rendez-vous.
drills.translation: She told me she had forgotten her appointment.

points.title: Reported speech (le discours indirect)
points.function: Report what someone said or asked
points.explanation: Report a statement with que: il dit qu'il vient 'he says he's coming'. Report a yes/no question with si 'whether': il demande si tu viens. Report what/who with ce que / ce qui: je ne sais pas ce qu'il veut. When the main verb is past, tenses shift back (présent → imparfait, etc.).

drills.translation: He says (that) he is tired.

drills.sentence: Elle demande {{answer}} tu veux un café.
drills.translation: She asks whether you want a coffee.

drills.sentence: Je ne sais pas ce {{answer}}'il veut.
drills.translation: I don't know what he wants.

drills.sentence: Il a dit qu'il {{answer}} le lendemain.
drills.translation: He said he would come the next day.
Tell me what happened.
She asked me whether I had finished.

points.title: The passive voice (la voix passive)
points.function: Say an action is done, and by whom
points.explanation: The passive = être (in any tense) + past participle, which AGREES with the subject: la lettre est écrite 'the letter is written', les maisons ont été construites 'the houses were built'. The agent, if named, follows par: le tableau a été peint par Monet.

drills.translation: The letter is written in French.

drills.sentence: Ce roman a {{answer}} traduit en dix langues.
drills.translation: This novel has been translated into ten languages.

drills.sentence: Les portes {{answer}} fermées à neuf heures.
drills.translation: The doors are closed at nine.

drills.sentence: Le tableau a été peint {{answer}} un artiste célèbre.
drills.translation: The painting was painted by a famous artist.

drills.sentence: La maison {{answer}} construite il y a cent ans.
drills.translation: The house was built a hundred years ago.
These decisions will be made tomorrow.

points.title: Hypotheticals with si (si + imparfait)
points.function: Say what would happen if…
points.explanation: For an unreal or hypothetical present, si + imparfait pairs with the conditional: si j'avais le temps, je viendrais 'if I had time, I would come'. (For a real future condition it's si + présent → futur: si tu études, tu réussiras.) Never put the conditional directly after si.

drills.sentence: Si j'avais le temps, je {{answer}} au cinéma.
drills.translation: If I had time, I would go to the cinema.
If I were rich, I would travel everywhere.

drills.sentence: Si nous {{answer}} plus tôt, nous verrions le lever du soleil.
drills.translation: If we left earlier, we would see the sunrise.
You would succeed if you worked more.

drills.sentence: S'il {{answer}} beau, nous ferions une promenade.
drills.translation: If the weather were nice, we would take a walk.

drills.sentence: Si on me le demandait, je {{answer}} oui.
drills.translation: If they asked me, I would say yes.

points.title: The gerund (le gérondif: en faisant)
points.function: Say 'while / by doing' something
points.explanation: The gérondif is en + the present participle (-ant): en marchant 'while walking', en travaillant 'by working'. It expresses simultaneity ('while') or means ('by'), and its subject is always the same as the main verb's: il chante en cuisinant 'he sings while cooking'.

drills.sentence: Il est tombé {{answer}} courant après le bus.
drills.translation: He fell while running for the bus.
You learn a lot by travelling.

drills.sentence: Elle écoute de la musique en {{answer}}.
drills.translation: She listens to music while cooking.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} lisant tous les jours, tu progresseras.
drills.translation: By reading every day, you'll improve.
He hurt himself while falling.
Don't talk while eating!

points.title: Subjunctive after conjunctions
points.function: Use the subjunctive after 'although, so that, before…'
points.explanation: Certain conjunctions always take the subjunctive: bien que / quoique 'although', pour que / afin que 'so that', avant que 'before', à condition que 'provided that', jusqu'à ce que 'until', sans que 'without'. Bien qu'il pleuve, nous sortons 'although it's raining, we go out'.

drills.sentence: Bien qu'il {{answer}} froid, ils se baignent.
drills.translation: Although it's cold, they swim.

drills.sentence: Je t'explique pour que tu {{answer}} comprendre.
drills.translation: I'm explaining so that you can understand.
Let's leave before it rains.

drills.sentence: Je viendrai à condition que tu {{answer}} là.
drills.translation: I'll come provided that you're there.

drills.sentence: Attends jusqu'à ce que je {{answer}} prêt.
drills.translation: Wait until I'm ready.

drills.sentence: Il est parti sans que je le {{answer}}.
drills.translation: He left without my knowing it.

points.title: Irregular comparatives (meilleur, mieux, pire)
points.function: Compare with 'better' and 'worse'
points.explanation: A few comparatives are irregular. The adjective bon → meilleur 'better' (agrees: meilleure, meilleurs). The adverb bien → mieux 'better' (never agrees). mauvais → plus mauvais or pire 'worse'; mal → plus mal or pis. Ce gâteau est meilleur; il chante mieux.

drills.translation: This restaurant is better than the other.
She sings better than me.
The situation is worse than before.
It's the best bakery in the neighbourhood.
Today I feel better than yesterday.
His results are worse than expected.

points.title: Logical connectors (pourtant, donc, cependant)
points.function: Link ideas: contrast, cause, and consequence
points.explanation: Written and spoken French links ideas with connectors: donc / alors 'so/therefore', pourtant / cependant / néanmoins 'however/yet', car / puisque 'because/since', d'ailleurs 'besides', en revanche 'on the other hand'. They organize an argument and are worth deploying deliberately.

drills.sentence: Il pleut, {{answer}} nous restons à la maison.
drills.translation: It's raining, so we're staying home.
She is tired; however, she carries on.

drills.sentence: Je reste ici {{answer}} j'attends un ami.
drills.translation: I'm staying here because I'm waiting for a friend.

drills.sentence: Il n'aime pas Paris; {{answer}}, il y travaille.
drills.translation: He doesn't like Paris; however, he works there.
Since you're ready, let's go.

drills.sentence: Le café est cher; {{answer}}, le thé est bon marché.
drills.translation: Coffee is expensive; however, tea is cheap.

points.title: The past conditional (le conditionnel passé)
points.function: Say what would have happened
points.explanation: The past conditional = the conditional of avoir/être + the participle: j'aurais fait 'I would have done', elle serait venue 'she would have come'. It pairs with si + plus-que-parfait for past-unreal conditions: si j'avais su, je serais venu 'if I had known, I would have come'.

drills.sentence: Si j'avais su, je {{answer}} venu plus tôt.
drills.translation: If I had known, I would have come earlier.
He would have succeeded if he had worked.
We would have liked to stay longer.

drills.sentence: Sans ton aide, je n'{{answer}} jamais fini.
drills.translation: Without your help, I would never have finished.

drills.sentence: Elles {{answer}} venues, mais il était trop tard.
drills.translation: They would have come, but it was too late.

drills.sentence: Qu'est-ce que tu {{answer}} fait à ma place?
drills.translation: What would you have done in my place?

points.title: The past subjunctive & sequence of tenses
points.function: Use the subjunctive for a completed action
points.explanation: The past subjunctive = the subjunctive of avoir/être + the participle, for an action seen as completed within a subjunctive context: je suis content que tu sois venu 'I'm glad you came', bien qu'il ait fait des erreurs 'although he made mistakes'. It replaces the present subjunctive when the action is already over.

drills.sentence: Je suis ravi que tu {{answer}} venu.
drills.translation: I'm delighted that you came.

drills.sentence: Bien qu'il {{answer}} fait de son mieux, il a échoué.
drills.translation: Although he did his best, he failed.

drills.sentence: C'est dommage qu'elle {{answer}} partie si tôt.
drills.translation: It's a shame she left so early.
I doubt they have already finished.

drills.sentence: Nous sommes contents que vous {{answer}} pu venir.
drills.translation: We're glad you were able to come.

drills.sentence: Il est possible qu'elle l'{{answer}} oublié.
drills.translation: It's possible she forgot it.

points.title: The literary past (le passé simple)
points.function: Recognize the tense of novels and history
points.explanation: The passé simple is the written narrative past, replacing the passé composé in literature, history, and formal storytelling. -er verbs: il parla, ils parlèrent; -ir/-re: il finit, il vendit; key irregulars: être → il fut, avoir → il eut, faire → il fit, venir → il vint. You rarely produce it, but you must read it.

drills.translation: The king built a great palace. (narrative)
They entered the dark forest. (narrative)

drills.sentence: Elle {{answer}} très surprise en le voyant. (récit)
drills.translation: She was very surprised on seeing him. (narrative)

drills.sentence: Le héros {{answer}} son épée et partit. (récit)
drills.translation: The hero took his sword and left. (narrative)

drills.sentence: Soudain, un étranger {{answer}} à la porte. (récit)
drills.translation: Suddenly, a stranger knocked at the door. (narrative)
They had a feast that evening. (narrative)

points.title: Emphasis (c'est… qui / que, ce qui… c'est)
points.function: Foreground a word with a cleft construction
points.explanation: French emphasizes a word by 'clefting' it. c'est … qui highlights a subject: c'est moi qui l'ai fait 'it's I who did it'. c'est … que highlights anything else: c'est à Paris que je vis 'it's in Paris that I live'. ce qui/ce que … c'est frames a whole idea: ce que je veux, c'est réussir 'what I want is to succeed'.

drills.sentence: C'est moi {{answer}} ai préparé le dîner.
drills.translation: It's I who prepared the dinner.

drills.sentence: C'est à Lyon {{answer}} je suis né.
drills.translation: It's in Lyon that I was born.

drills.sentence: Ce {{answer}} je veux, c'est un peu de calme.
drills.translation: What I want is a bit of quiet.

drills.sentence: Ce {{answer}} me plaît, c'est sa sincérité.
drills.translation: What pleases me is his sincerity.
It's you (whom) I was looking for.

drills.sentence: C'est ce livre-là {{answer}} il faut lire.
drills.translation: It's that book you must read.

points.title: Elevated register (subjonctif littéraire, ne explétif)
points.function: Recognize and use the formal written layer
points.explanation: Formal/literary French keeps forms speech has dropped. The 'ne explétif' adds a meaningless ne after avant que, de peur que, and comparisons: je crains qu'il ne vienne 'I fear he will come'. Elevated register also prefers connectors like néanmoins, toutefois, quoique, en dépit de, and the inverted question (viendrez-vous?).

drills.sentence: Je crains qu'il {{answer}} vienne trop tard.
drills.translation: I fear he will come too late. (ne explétif)

drills.sentence: Partez avant qu'il {{answer}} soit trop tard.
drills.translation: Leave before it's too late. (ne explétif)

drills.sentence: Il a échoué; {{answer}}, il ne se décourage pas.
drills.translation: He failed; nevertheless, he isn't discouraged.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} il soit tard, poursuivons la discussion.
drills.translation: Although it's late, let's continue the discussion.

drills.sentence: En {{answer}} de la pluie, la fête a eu lieu.
drills.translation: In spite of the rain, the party took place.
Will you attend tomorrow's conference?
